title: Psalms 34
subtitle: Taste and See That the LORD Is Good

sections.header: Verses 1–10 — “I Sought the LORD and He Answered Me”

heb.paragraph: "Taste (ṭaʿămū) and see that the LORD is good; blessed is the one who takes refuge in him" (v.8). An invitation to experiential knowledge. God’s goodness is not argued but tasted — proven through direct encounter. Peter quotes this in 1 Peter 2:3.

refs.note: "Like newborn babies, crave pure spiritual milk... now that you have tasted that the Lord is good" — Peter quoting Psalm 34:8.
David pretends to be insane before Abimelek/Achish — the superscription’s historical context.

notes.note: "I sought the LORD, and he answered me; he delivered me from all my fears." Simple testimony: sought, found, delivered.
"Taste and see that the LORD is good." Not "believe and see" but "taste and see." Experiential, sensory, personal. You have to try it to know it.
"The lions may grow weak and hungry, but those who seek the LORD lack no good thing." Even the mightiest predators experience lack. God’s seekers do not.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the imperative is plural: taste (you all) and see. The invitation is communal, not just individual. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.

Calvin: "taste and see" is the invitation to every uncertain soul. Calvin: you do not need to understand God fully before you experience God really. Taste first; theology follows. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: ṭaʿămū (taste) is the same verb used for Jonathan tasting honey (1 Sam 14:29). The analogy is physical: as honey proves its sweetness on the tongue, God proves his goodness to the seeker. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "taste" is a daring word for theology. It demands experience, not just assent. God’s goodness is verifiable by encounter. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the psalm alternates between testimony (what God did for David) and instruction (what God will do for all who seek). Private experience becomes public teaching. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "taste and see" bridges the gap between information and knowledge. You can know about food; tasting it is different. Theology becomes doxology through experience. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: An acrostic psalm. David praises God at all times (v.1), tells of seeking and finding (v.4), testifies that the afflicted are heard (v.6), and issues the famous invitation: taste and see (v.8). "The lions may grow weak and hungry, but those who seek the LORD lack no good thing" (v.10).

sections.header: Verses 11–22 — “The LORD Is Close to the Brokenhearted”

heb.paragraph: "The LORD is close to the brokenhearted (nishbərē-lēb) and saves those who are crushed in spirit" (v.18). The most comforting verse in the Psalter for the suffering. God’s proximity is inversely proportional to human wholeness: the more broken you are, the closer God comes.

refs.note: "Not one of his bones will be broken" — applied to Christ at the crucifixion.
Peter quotes Psalm 34:12–16 for the Christian ethical life.
"He has sent me to bind up the brokenhearted" — Christ’s mission defined by this psalm’s theology.

notes.note: "The LORD is close to the brokenhearted and saves those who are crushed in spirit." The promise: God’s presence intensifies in proportion to human brokenness. The most shattered person is nearest to God.
"The righteous person may have many troubles, but the LORD delivers him from them all." Not from HAVING troubles but from being destroyed BY them. Troubles are many; deliverances match.
"He protects all his bones, not one of them will be broken." Applied to Christ in John 19:36 — not a bone broken despite crucifixion.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "close" (qārōb) is spatial language for relational reality. God draws near physically, as it were, to the emotionally devastated. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.
Calvin: the brokenhearted have one advantage: they have stopped pretending. Calvin: God is close to the broken because the broken have nothing left to hide.
Calvin: the unbroken-bones promise finds its ultimate fulfilment at the cross. Calvin: the soldiers broke the legs of the two criminals but not Christ’s. Psalm 34:20 was being kept.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: nishbərē-lēb (broken of heart) uses the same root as "broken pottery" in Psalm 31:12. The breakage is real, not metaphorical.
The NET: John 19:36 cites this verse as fulfilled in Christ’s crucifixion. The Passover lamb’s unbroken bones (Exod 12:46) and this psalm’s promise converge at the cross.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "close to the brokenhearted" reverses every expectation. God is not close to the successful, the strong, or the proud. He is close to the broken. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the psalm’s theology of divine proximity to brokenness is foundational for pastoral care. God models where the church should be: near the shattered. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "crushed in spirit" (dak̅ʾē-rūaḥ) is the opposite of the proud spirit God resists. Brokenness is the qualification for nearness. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: David teaches the fear of the LORD (v.11), which consists in guarding the tongue, turning from evil, doing good, and seeking peace (vv.13–14). The LORD’s eyes and ears attend to the righteous (v.15). "The righteous person may have many troubles, but the LORD delivers him from them all" (v.19). "He protects all his bones, not one of them will be broken" (v.20) — John 19:36 applies this to Christ on the cross.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: Fugitive who tasted God's deliverance at Gath (superscription, vv.1-8)
ppl.text: The superscription places this when David 'feigned insanity before Abimelech' (1 Sam 21:10-15, where the Philistine king is called Achish — Abimelech may be a royal title). David's acrostic wisdom psalm transforms personal terror into public instruction: 'I sought the LORD, and he answered me; he delivered me from all my fears' (v.4). 'Taste and see that the LORD is good' (v.8) is embodied knowledge — David invites others into his own experience of divine rescue.

ppl.name: God (Yahweh)
ppl.role: Deliverer of the brokenhearted, guardian of the righteous (vv.7,15-22)
ppl.text: The angel of the LORD 'encamps around those who fear him' (v.7) — military imagery for divine protection. God is 'close to the brokenhearted' and 'saves those who are crushed in spirit' (v.18) — proximity increases with suffering, not success. 'He protects all his bones, not one of them will be broken' (v.20) is applied to Christ's crucifixion in John 19:36, making this psalm part of the Passion narrative's scriptural scaffolding.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVGlorify the Lord with me; let us exalt his name together.ESVOh, magnify the LORD with me, and let us exalt his name together!NIVI will glory in the Lord; let the afflicted hear and rejoice.ESVMy soul makes its boast in the LORD; let the humble hear and be glad.

rec.text: ""I sought the LORD, and he answered me; he delivered me from all my fears." Simple testimony: sought, found, delivered."

rows.label: Close to the Broken — 34:11–22
rows.text: "The LORD is close to the brokenhearted." Many troubles, many deliverances. Not one bone broken.

hebtext.note: Peter alludes to Psalm 34:8 in 1 Peter 2:3, saying 'if indeed you have tasted that the Lord is good' - this is an allusion rather than a direct quotation.

"The LORD is close to the brokenhearted (nishbərē-lēb) and saves those who are crushed in spirit" (v.18). The most comforting verse in the Psalter for the suffering. God’s proximity is inversely proportional to human wholeness: the more broken you are, the closer God comes.

themes.note: Mercy peaks: close to the brokenhearted, taste-and-see theology. Faith: experiential knowledge of God’s goodness. Prophecy: unbroken bones fulfilled at the cross.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on 'Taste and see that the LORD is good,' consider asking God whether your knowledge of His goodness is experiential or theoretical — whether you have actually tasted it or only heard others describe the flavor.
